does: This paper investigates vulnerabilities in machine unlearning services offered by MLaaS providers, focusing on the phenomenon of over-unlearning where more information is removed from a model than necessary. The authors develop two novel attack strategies that exploit over-unlearning to expose and measure degradation in the utility-privacy trade-off that unlearning methods must balance. These strategies operate under black-box access constraints, making them practical against real MLaaS systems where attackers cannot inspect model internals. Through systematic experiments on benchmark datasets with multiple model architectures and representative unlearning approaches, the work demonstrates that over-unlearning attacks achieve significant success in quantifying vulnerabilities in current unlearning services.
connects: This work extends the emerging literature on machine unlearning, which has grown from theoretical GDPR right-to-be-forgotten frameworks toward practical implementations in MLaaS settings. It builds on prior work examining the privacy-utility trade-offs inherent in approximate unlearning methods, where perfect data removal often comes at prohibitive computational cost. The paper connects to the broader threat model literature in machine learning security, particularly black-box attacks and membership inference variants that assess privacy risks without model access. It also engages with the tension between regulatory requirements for data deletion and the technical realities of keeping training data inaccessible to service providers, extending prior analyses of unlearning robustness and the practical limitations of retrofit deletion approaches.
enables: This work establishes a systematic framework for auditing and testing machine unlearning services, pointing toward improved verification methods for GDPR and privacy regulation compliance. The identified over-unlearning vulnerability suggests future unlearning methods must be designed with additional robustness guarantees against degradation-based attacks. The findings motivate development of better utility-preservation techniques in approximate unlearning and could inform design of MLaaS architectures that provide stronger assurances about unlearning quality. This research enables practitioners to empirically validate unlearning claims and supports the creation of benchmarks for measuring unlearning service reliability, laying groundwork for trustworthy machine unlearning systems that balance regulatory compliance with model utility.
